title: Why Did the Angels Prostrate to Adam and Not Eve? | Q&A with Nouman Ali Khan
description:
How could Iblis stand in the presence of Allah and still refuse His command? In this Q&A, Ustadh Nouman Ali Khan answers three questions. The first: why were the angels commanded to prostrate to Adam (AS) and not to Eve as well? Ustadh Nouman explains that Adam (AS) is the first human being, not merely the first man, and that all of his children, including Eve (Hawwa), were honored through that moment. The second: did Iblis lose his awe of Allah? The answer is a psychological diagnosis: his entire self-worth was built on comparison, and the moment he was no longer number one, his desire to protect his image became greater than his recognition of Allah. The third: do we need to master Arabic to understand the Quran? Everyone is on a journey with the Word of Allah, and that is exactly what He wants.

title: What Backbiting Is and Why Muslims Still Do It | Surah Al-Hujurat | Nouman Ali Khan
description:
In this clip from our Deeper Look of Surah Al-Hujurat, Ustadh Nouman Ali Khan unpacks one of the most overlooked dimensions of Ayah 49:12, the verse in which Allah asks whether any of you would love to eat the flesh of your dead brother and you would despise that. Most people hear this verse and think about the person doing the backbiting. Ustadh Nouman shows why that reading is incomplete. 

The grammatical choice Allah makes, addressing the prohibition in the third person rather than directly, is deliberate: it covers not only the one speaking but every person sitting in the room who heard it and said nothing. Silence, in this Ayah, is a violation. 

He then walks through the real reasons people backbite or permit it around them: unaddressed anger, the fear of social awkwardness, a need to deflect attention from their own flaws and jealousy toward others who appear happy. The image Allah chose, eating your own brother's dead flesh, is so viscerally repulsive precisely because our tongues produce harm we have grown numb to. The ugliness of the image is the point. Watch the full Deeper Look episode and the complete series at https://byna.tv/21.

title: What to Do When Someone Never Forgives You
description:
Not everyone who owes you an apology will offer one, and not everyone you apologize to will forgive you. In this reflection, Ustadh Nouman Ali Khan addresses one of the hardest truths about forgiveness: it is not up to you, and it never was. Some people will call you an angel, others will call you a devil for the exact same mistake, and neither verdict changes who you actually are before Allah. Ustadh Nouman Ali Khan draws a clear line between people's judgment and Allah's judgment, one shaped by anger and limited patience, the other rooted in perfect wisdom, justice and mercy. He explains what it means to seek forgiveness sincerely, do your part, then release the outcome, trusting that Allah compensates what people withhold. Let this be the moment you hand that weight over to the only judgment that truly counts.

------------------------------

title: What’s the point of Jesus’ Miracle Birth? | Q&A with Nouman Ali Khan
description:
Why would Allah design the miraculous birth of Isa (AS) in a way that invited people's worst assumptions? In this Q&A, Ustadh Nouman Ali Khan responds to a questioner reflecting on Surah Maryam (19:20), when Maryam (AS) asks how she could have a child when no man has touched her. Ustadh Nouman explains that the test was never really about her. It was a test for the people around her: a believing community that was quick to demonize, chastise and humiliate one of their own without knowing the facts. Allah flipped their assumptions, showing that honor comes from Him alone, and that Maryam (AS) and her son would become two of the most honored human beings in existence. Remarkably, Allah told her not to defend herself, because the more they spoke, the more they exposed themselves. A powerful lesson for anyone who has ever been judged unfairly.

title: Why Reading Into What People Say Poisons Your Heart | Surah Al-Hujurat | Nouman Ali Khan
description:
In this clip from our Deeper Look of Surah Al-Hujurat, Ustadh Nouman Ali Khan unpacks a tension that quietly corrodes Muslim communities: the habit of making bad assumptions about people who have given you no reason to doubt them. Drawing from the Ayah يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اجْتَنِبُوا كَثِيرًا مِّنَ الظَّنِّ إِنَّ بَعْضَ الظَّنِّ إِثْمٌ وَلَا تَجَسَّسُوا (49:12), Ustadh Nouman walks through the scholarly position that suspicion toward someone whose outward conduct is good is simply not permitted and that suspicion left unchecked leads naturally to spying. 

He brings a hadith to life in which the Prophet ﷺ stood before the Ka’bah and swore that the dignity, blood and wealth of a Muslim are more sacred to Allah than the Ka’bah itself, meaning that attacking a believer's honor through assumption is graver than desecrating that sacred house. The clip closes with a practical warning: obsessing over what people really meant when they spoke to you is not cleverness, it is a failure of trust in Allah. Watch the full Deeper Look series on Bayyinah TV at https://byna.tv/21.

title: "At Least I Said It to Your Face"
description:
We think honesty is a virtue. And it is. But calling someone out on their flaws, to their face, isn't honesty. It's humiliation.
 
Ayesha (RA) once made a small gesture about another person's appearance. Just a gesture. No words.
The Prophet ﷺ  said: "You have contaminated your words with it. If that word was thrown into the ocean, the entire ocean would be contaminated."
 
One small gesture. One mocking look. And it poisons everything.
 
Watch your words. Watch your gestures. Watch your eyes.

------------------------------

title: Why Some Sahabah Finished the Quran in a Week and Others Didn't
description:
Not every companion of the Prophet ﷺ read the Quran the same way. Some completed it in a week. Some took ten days. Others read a little every single day. So which one is the standard?
In this reflection, Ustadh Nouman Ali Khan explains why comparing your Quran habits to the most remarkable stories from history sets an unfair bar. The cases that get recorded in the books are often the exceptions, not the norm, and history has to be read in balance, without hyperbole. He draws this back to the closing verses of Surah Al-Muzzammil, where Allah acknowledges that not everyone can do the same amount, so recite what is easy for you of the Quran (73:20).
If your Ramadan Quran goal feels smaller than someone else's, this is a reminder that consistency matters more than comparison.

------------------------------

title: Selective Outrage: Standing Up Against Injustice | Q&A with Nouman Ali Khan
description:
It's easy to speak up about injustice happening a thousand miles away. It's much harder when it's happening in your own family. In this Q&A, Ustadh Nouman Ali Khan responds to a questioner struggling to understand how to live by the command in Surah Al-Ma'idah (5:8): stand up for Allah as witnesses to justice, and never let hatred keep you from being fair. Ustadh Nouman explains that justice is not only about geopolitics and activism. The same Allah who commanded us to pray and fast commanded us to speak up when we see wrong, whether it's financial injustice, family injustice or wrongdoing among our own friends. Staying silent to avoid drama while posting about causes far away is a double standard the Quran does not allow. The goal is a real commitment to the actual word of Allah and what it does in our lives.

title: Your Sarcastic Compliment Is Still an Insult | Surah Al-Hujurat | Nouman Ali Khan
description:
In this clip from Surah Al-Hujurat Deeper Look Series Ustadh Nouman Ali Khan walks through one of the most overlooked forms of harm in Muslim communities: the words we say to each other's faces, the assumptions we hold in our hearts and the nicknames we throw around as jokes.

He breaks down the Arabic distinction between a derogatory nickname (namas) and a harmless title (laqab), and explains why Allah warns us against even sarcastic use of kind words. He also discusses a narration from Aisha (RA) where the Prophet ﷺ told her that a single gesture of mockery contaminated her entire speech and could have contaminated an ocean. 

The clip closes with a powerful reminder that bad assumptions toward another believer are not just a social problem: they are backbiting of the heart and one of the rare sins in Islam where the thought itself is held accountable.

title: How Long Will My Oppressors Keep Getting Away With It? | Q&A with Nouman Ali Khan
description:
What do you do when someone with more power, more leverage and more rights keeps hurting you, and there seem to be no consequences for them at all? Ustadh Nouman Ali Khan answers a question many Muslims quietly carry: the feeling that injustice goes unanswered and that nothing will ever change.

He explains that the power an oppressor holds is given by Allah and can be taken back by Him at any moment. تُؤْتِي الْمُلْكَ مَن تَشَاءُ وَتَنزِعُ الْمُلْكَ مِمَّن تَشَاءُ (3:26). Those who think their cruelty has put them ahead are making a horrible calculation and what they hoard and withhold will become a collar around their necks on Judgment Day (3:180).

The relief Allah promises is real, but its timing and form belong to Him, not to us. That is no excuse for cowardice. We are still called to stand up to the tyrant, even when we are small, and leave the rest in Allah's hands.

title: Why People of Effort Never Burn Out
description:
Why do so many of us feel broken when we don't see results, no matter how hard we work? Ustadh Nouman Ali Khan describes a different kind of person: one who isn't crushed by the absence of results because they are sustained by the effort itself.
Using the feeling after a hard workout, he explains how sincere effort carries its own motivation before any final goal is reached. A day of diligent, honest work is a successful day, worth celebrating on its own. This mindset keeps you from living trapped in the future you haven't reached or the past you regret, and roots you firmly in the present.

title: The Taqwa Nobody Talks About | Nouman Ali Khan
description:
We think taqwa is between us and Allah. Prayer, fasting, worship, purely spiritual.

But these ayat flip that understanding completely.

Making peace between believers is taqwa. Fixing your relationships is taqwa. How you treat people around you is how you show your fear and consciousness of Allah.

And there's a warning too, even when your intentions are good, the ends don't justify the means. Don't cross lines in the name of doing good.

Taqwa isn't just what happens on your prayer mat. It shows up in how you deal with people.

------------------------------

title: Brotherhood Has No Madhab | Nouman Ali Khan
description:
Allah described the believers as absolutely humble before one another. Not just respectful, humbled. A bond thicker than blood.

But today? We've replaced that with madhabs, schools of thought, institutes, and sheikhs. We put a hundred qualifications before we'll call someone our brother.

"إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِخْوَةٌ", The believers are nothing but brothers.

That's it. No conditions. No labels. Just La ilaha illa Allah and before you stands your Muslim brother.

We had the most powerful bond in the world. And we made it weak.

------------------------------

title: You Can't Have Taqwa and Ignore People | Surah Al-Hujurat | Nouman Ali Khan
description:
In this clip from Surah Al-Hujurat Deeper Look Series, Ustadh Nouman Ali Khan talks about one of the most striking claims in the Quran: that true believers are nothing but blood brothers. A bond defined not by ethnicity, language, school of thought or circle, but by iman alone.

Ustadh Nouman walks through the ayah وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ (49:10) and explains why making peace between believers is not optional goodwill but an act of taqwa itself. He shares a hadith about aiding both the wrongdoer and the wronged, explaining that helping someone who is in the wrong means stopping them from wrongdoing, that is how you truly help them. He also reflects on the price of fairness: a truly fair judgment often leaves everyone unhappy and yet Allah says He loves those who avoid unfairness.

The clip closes with a direct, honest challenge about how Muslims today filter brotherhood through nationality, madhab, wealth, immigration status and even the way someone wears hijab and why that, according to this surah, is the very definition of jahaliyyah.

title: Why People of Effort Are Never Broken by Failure
description:
There's a difference between not caring about results and not being broken when the results don't come. People of effort care deeply, but their hearts aren't crushed by what they can't control. The effort itself is what sustains them.
In this clip, Ustadh Nouman Ali Khan draws a striking parallel to something many of us know well. Anyone who works out understands that finishing the session is a win, even before the body transforms. That feeling, this was worth it, this day was a success, is exactly the mindset the Quran trains in us.
When you celebrate sincere effort right now, you stop living in the regret of the past or the anxiety of the future. You live in the present, anchored by Alhamdulillah and the work in front of you. Even modern conversations about mental health circle back to what Islam taught all along.
Let's become people of effort, and celebrate it.
